issue_title: Parsing datetime objects doesn't parse their timezone
issue_body:
# Setup

``` toml
[section]
date = 2016-02-24T13:05:09Z
```

Note the Z on the end: it's saying that this datetime is in Zulu time, or UTC.

# Observed behavior

``` python
>>> with open('test.toml', 'r') as fp:
...     data = toml.loads(fp.read())
... 
>>> data['section']['date']
datetime.datetime(2016, 2, 24, 13, 5, 9)
```

# Expected behavior

``` python
>>> data['section']['date']
datetime.datetime(2016, 2, 24, 13, 5, 9, tzinfo=<UTC>)
```

Per the [TOML spec](https://github.com/toml-lang/toml#user-content-datetime), it would be fine to return naive objects if no TZ were specified, but in the case that the TZ is in fact specified, I'd like to receive a TZ-aware object.

issue_title: Consider improving the parameter configuration interface
issue_body:
Currently the parameter configuration is being set in this way:
```
        self.xlow = -15 * np.ones(dim)
        self.xup = 20 * np.ones(dim)
        self.integer = []
        self.continuous = np.arange(0, dim)
```

This is very cumbersome interface, especially when you have 15 parameters, each with different ranges, and are of different type. It's very easy to make a mistake.
Further if the type of parameter is left unassigned, there is no error or warning. Example

```
      self.integer = [0, 1, 2]
      self.continuous = [4, 5]
```

Here the fourth parameter is left without a type (integer or continuous), but there is no error or warning produced, and the algorithm just considers it as continuous. 

Also, it would be useful if we can assign a name to a parameter. 

I suggest to adopt some JSON configuration file, similar to what other optimization algorithms use. Example:
```
       {
           parameters:[
                      {
                          name: "learning_rate",
                          low: 10,
                          high: 20,
                          type: "Integer"
                      },
                     ...
           ]
       }
```
Most importantly produce an error or at least a warning if not all of the configuration properties are set. 

Thanks

issue_title: Adding comment with new line generates error
Dependency on grep command which is missing on Windows by default
issue_body:
The problem arises in the `_get_valid_formats()` function in `pysox/sox/core.py`.

I propose a fix as follows:
```
def _get_valid_formats():
    ''' Calls SoX help for a lists of audio formats available with the current
    install of SoX.
    Returns:
    --------
    formats : list
        List of audio file extensions that SoX can process.
    '''
    if NO_SOX:
        return []

    pfm = platform.system()
    if pfm == 'Windows':
        shell_output = subprocess.check_output('sox -h | findstr /c:"AUDIO FILE FORMATS"', shell=True)
    else:
        shell_output = subprocess.check_output('sox -h | grep "AUDIO FILE FORMATS"', shell=True)

    formats = shell_output.decode('utf8').split()[3:] # this is better because it does not leave whitespace characters in the string
    return formats
```
This issue tracks adding support for python 3

Hello,

Just wanted you to know that on python2 `bytes(2)` returns the string `"2"`, whereas in python3 it returns `b"\x00\x00"`.

This makes the padding (inside `H()`) wrong in python2 which results in invalid values.

PySocks fails when used with `asyncore`:

* asyncore uses `connect_ex` instead of `connect` which is not implemented by PySocks and silently calls `socket.connect_ex`
* `socksocket.connect` fails with non blocking sockets.

I managed to make it work on Linux by re-implementing asyncore connect and create_socket. The code is a draft and could be cleaned up.

> https://github.com/iot-lab/aggregation-tools/commit/c0fa46d775999785c116cb9b14cd008f27d53b9c#diff-08168129224d3895b73aa8df60b11a26L63

For connect_ex I just called connect and catched the exceptions and tried to handle them. Maybe just implementing connect_hex that return 0 or ERROR would be enough for asyncore, even if not all errors are correctly implemented.

I then got the issues with non blocking sockets. Looking at the implementation it's normal that it fails when connecting.
However just passing it 'blocking' for the connection time would be enough to be usable directly,
and then restore the previous configuration after connecting.

issue_title: Look into enabling LAPACK for Fortran matmul intrinsic
issue_body:
See some [discussion][2] on StackOverflow. Options, e.g.:

```
-fblas-matmul-limit
-fblas-matmul-limit  =30
-finline-matmul-limit=30
```
From `-fblas-matmul-limit` [docs][1]

> If the matrices involved are not square, the size comparison is performed using the geometric mean of the dimensions of the argument and result matrices.

Possible BLAS implementations to install (on Debian):

```
$ apt install libblas3           # reference BLAS
$ apt install libopenblas-base   # openBLAS
$ apt install libatlas3-base     # ATLAS
```

[1]: https://gcc.gnu.org/onlinedocs/gcc-4.6.2/gfortran/Code-Gen-Options.html
[2]: https://stackoverflow.com/a/31494509/1068170

Hello @hackebrot,

I'm trying out poyo as a replacement for pyyaml in one of [my projects](https://github.com/gvalkov/tailon). The project's config file looks more or less like this:

```yaml
bind: 0.0.0.0:8081      # address and port to bind on
allow-transfers: true   # allow log file downloads
relative-root: /        # web app root path (default: '')
commands: [tail, grep]  # allowed commands
wrap-lines: true        # initial line-wrapping state

files:
  - 'logs/apache/www.tailon.org/'
```

This currently breaks poyo in two places:

1) Poyo tries to parse `0.0.0.0:8081` as a float.  Here's the debug output I added to `read_from_tag() `:

```
pattern: '(?P<quotes>[\'\\"]?).*(?P=quotes)'
group:  'bind'
string: 'bind'

pattern: '([-+]?(\\.[0-9]+|[0-9]+(\\.[0-9]*)?)([eE][-+]?[0-9]+)?)'
group:  '0.0'
string: '0.0.0.0:8081'
```

Perhaps the regex can be fixed or `read_from_tag()` can continue trying patterns until the one that matches most 'fully' is found?

2) The `relative-root: /        # web app root path (default: '')` line breaks the parser, because of the `:` in the comment:
```
pattern: '(?P<quotes>[\'\\"]?).*(?P=quotes)'
group:  'relative-root: "/"    # web app root path (default'
string: 'relative-root: "/"    # web app root path (default'

pattern: '(?P<quotes>[\'\\"]?).*(?P=quotes)'
group:  "''"
string: "''"
```

Kind regards,
Georgi

I've looked through the documentation, and I've also glanced at the source code, so my apologies if I've missed this feature, but could we get methods that allow us to iterate through the nodes and/or edges that have been added to an existing `Dot` object?

Off the top of my head, one way to implement this would to have attributes of `_node_list` and `_edge_list` that are initialized at `[]`. They're then updated when adding nodes and edges, and we can tack on a `nodes` method to return `_node_list` and allow calling the `edges` method with no (user-supplied) arguments to get a simple iterator of edge data, eg:

```
def node(self, name, label=None, _attributes=None, **attrs):
        """Create a node.
        Args:
            name: Unique identifier for the node inside the source.
            label: Caption to be displayed (defaults to the node name).
            attrs: Any additional node attributes (must be strings).
        """
        name = self.quote(name)
        attributes = self.attributes(label, attrs, _attributes)
        self.body.append(self._node % (name, attributes))
        node_dict = {"name": name, "label": label}
        self._nodes.append({"name": name, "label": label, "attributes": attrs})

def nodes(self):
    """Provides a list of node data (in the form of dicts) for nodes that have been added."""
    return self._nodes

def edge(self, tail_name, head_name, label=None, _attributes=None, **attrs):
        """Create an edge between two nodes.
        Args:
            tail_name: Start node identifier.
            head_name: End node identifier.
            label: Caption to be displayed near the edge.
            attrs: Any additional edge attributes (must be strings).
        """
        tail_name = self.quote_edge(tail_name)
        head_name = self.quote_edge(head_name)
        attributes = self.attributes(label, attrs, _attributes)
        edge = self._edge % (tail_name, head_name, attributes)
        self.body.append(edge)
        self._edges.append({"tail": tail_name, "head": head_name, "attributes": attrs})

def edges(self, tail_head_iter=None):
        """Create a bunch of edges or iterate through existing edges.
        Args:
            tail_head_iter: Iterable of (tail_name, head_name) pairs or None.
                If not provided, then a list of edge data is returned.
        """
        if tail_head_iter is not None:
            edge = self._edge_plain
            quote = self.quote_edge
            tail_head_iter = [(quote(t), quote(h)) for t, h in tail_head_iter]
            self.body.extend(edge % (t, h)
                for t, h in tail_head_iter)
            self._edges.extend([{"tail": t, "head": h} for t, h in tail_head_iter])
        else:
            return self._edges
```

This is probably a bit incorrect, as `_attributes` and `attrs` probably need to be merged. Now that I think about it, I'm not sure why there are two arguments that seem to provide the same thing (`_attributes` is supposed to be an object, right?). Also, we could be fancier and have separate `Node` and `Edge` objects to hold this data instead of `dict`s, but I'm not sure that's terribly necessary.

I'd be willing to make a pull request if this feature would be welcome.

Since JSON is the default serilization format for TinyDB there's the datetime problem:

```python
>>> from tinydb import TinyDB
>>> from datetime import datetime
>>> db = TinyDB("db.json")
>>> db.insert({"date": datetime.now()})
...
TypeError: datetime.datetime(2015, 2, 21, 17, 24, 17, 828569) is not JSON serializable
```

Many other databases handle datetime conversion for the user, and I would very much like TinyDB to do the same (It's usually fixed by specifying a [custom encoding](http://stackoverflow.com/questions/10721409/why-does-json-serialization-of-datetime-objects-in-python-not-work-out-of-the-bo) and a corresponding decoder when reading from the database).

Do you think this is a good idea?

To the reader: I don't think the current implementation will break, it is just far from optimal. Still, you may want to steer clear for now.

One way to handle this might be to call/construct TextBlob's parse with the pattern.en.parse() relation arg set to True. This would be especially good for functionality not provided by TextBlob.

E.G. pattern.en.parse() has the following options: 
parse(string, 
   tokenize = True,         # Split punctuation marks from words?
       tags = True,         # Parse part-of-speech tags? (NN, JJ, ...)
     chunks = True,         # Parse chunks? (NP, VP, PNP, ...)

issue_title: psutil only returns uids of user-owned process on Solaris 10
issue_body:
How to reproduce:

As normal user, chose a PID of another user:

    >>> p = psutil.Process(12345)
    >>> p.uids()
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 346, in wrapper
        return fun(self, *args, **kwargs)
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 477, in uids
        real, effective, saved, _, _, _ = self._proc_cred()
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_common.py", line 336, in wrapper
        return fun(self)
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 400, in _proc_cred
        return cext.proc_cred(self.pid, self._procfs_path)
    PermissionError: [Errno 13] Permission denied: '/proc/12345/cred'
     
    During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:
     
    Traceback (most recent call last):
      File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_common.py", line 336, in wrapper
        return fun(self)
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/__init__.py", line 768, in uids
        return self._proc.uids()
      File "/path/to/venv/lib/python3.5/site-packages/psutil/_pssunos.py", line 362, in wrapper
        raise AccessDenied(self.pid, self._name)
    psutil.AccessDenied: psutil.AccessDenied (pid=12345)

The thing is that psutil tries to read the uids from `/proc/$pid/cred` that is always only user-readable on Solaris 10:

    $ ls /proc/12345/cred -l
    -r-------- 1 juser juser 52 2017-11-23 11:27 /proc/12345/cred

A better choice on Solaris 10 is to use `/proc/$pid/psinfo` since it [also contains the uids](https://docs.oracle.com/cd/E19253-01/816-5174/proc-4/index.html) (modulo the saved user id) - and is readable by everyone. Thus, if psutil really cares about the saved user id, it could also try `/proc/$pid/cred` first and then fall back to `/proc/$pid/psinfo`.

For our example:

    $ ls /proc/12345/psinfo -l
    -r--r--r-- 1 juser juser 336 2017-11-23 11:27 /proc/12345/psinfo

issue_title: CPU usage for short lived processes
issue_body:
I recently had to diagnose a problem with high CPU usage. Running top, htop and glances was not helpful because the problem processes were too short lived to appear in the output.

A sys admin found the problem by running ````ps aux | sort -rk 3,3 | head -n 30````. This showed a number of ````ls```` commands running. Each was taking up about 8% of a CPU. Our application was spawning 400 ````ls```` commands per second. It was an ````ls```` on an empty directory so each process didn't run long.

I was able to find some of these commands with psutil but I only got 0% when calling the cpu_percent() method. How could the ````ps```` command get the CPU percentage for these processes but psutil can't?

issue_title: RFE: return max/peak RSS usage from Process.memory_info
issue_body:
The peak memory usage is an important parameter to monitor.
It is available as the *nonstandard* `ru_maxrss` field in [`struct rusage`](http://pubs.opengroup.org/onlinepubs/9699919799/basedefs/sys_resource.h.html) on most UNIX platforms (although in different units: bytes on BSD, kilobytes on Linux), and you are already returning it from `memory_info` on Windows as `peak_wset`.
It would be nice if you could add it with a consistent cross-platform name and units (like you already do with `rss` and `vms`) to the `pmem` structure on all platforms.
The name should probably be  `peak_rss` and the units should be bytes (for consistency with `rss`).
Thank you!

I like this tokenizer better than using `tokenize`.

 I thought standard tokenize module would be optimized in C, but it's fully written in Python and since it has so much more than we actually need, I'm sure it's not as efficient. Furthermore, I'd go as far as yielding the token, tokstr and the position, it being either an offset or a (row, col) pair, which for most cases would be enough:

```python
def tokenizer(expr, symbol_class=boolean.Symbol):
    if not isinstance(expr, basestring):
        raise TypeError("expr must be string but it is %s." % type(expr))

    TOKENS = {
        '*': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        '&': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        'and': boolean.TOKEN_AND,
        '|': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        '+': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        'or': boolean.TOKEN_OR,
        '~': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        '!': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        'not': boolean.TOKEN_NOT,
        '(': boolean.TOKEN_LPAR,
        '[': boolean.TOKEN_LPAR,
        ']': boolean.TOKEN_RPAR,
        ')': boolean.TOKEN_RPAR,
        'true': boolean.TRUE,
        '1': boolean.TRUE,
        'false': boolean.FALSE,
        '0': boolean.FALSE,
        'none': boolean.FALSE,
    }

    length = len(expr)
    offset = 0
    while offset < length:
        tok = expr[offset]

        sym = tok.isalpha() or tok == '_'
        if sym:
            offset += 1
            while offset < length:
                char = expr[offset]
                if char.isalnum() or char in ('.', ':', '_'):
                    offset += 1
                    tok += char
                else:
                    break
            offset -= 1

        try:
            yield TOKENS[tok.lower()], tok, offset
        except KeyError:
            if sym:
                yield symbol_class(tok), tok, offset
            elif tok not in (' ', '\t', '\r', '\n'):
                raise TypeError("Unknown token %s at position %s." % (tok, offset))

        offset += 1
```

This prevents the use of `tokenize` and `StringIO`, and to me is easier to understand, since it's so simple. Users can always use their own tokenizers anyway.

issue_title: Non-ASCII characters in connection string password cause UnicodeEncodeError in cleanup
issue_body:
This script successfully connects to a SQL Server database using a login with a non-ASCII character in the password, but causes a `UnicodeEncodeError` when garbage collecting the pyodbc connection (I assume) and crashes python:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# encoding: utf-8
from __future__ import unicode_literals, print_function

import gc
import sys

import pyodbc

# Please ensure these are valid parameters for your test SQL Server - if pyodbc
# cannot establish a connection then the bug seems to be avoided.
CONN_PARTS = {
    'DRIVER': '{SQL Server}',
    'Server': '.\\SQLEXPRESS',
    'DATABASE': 'test_pyodbc',
    'UID': 'test_pyodbc_unicode',

    # PWD must contain Unicode that cannot be encoded as ASCII to invoke crash.
    'PWD': 'pa££word',
}
CONN_STR = ";".join(key + '=' + val for key, val in CONN_PARTS.items())

def main():
    print('python version:', sys.version)
    print('pyodbc version:', pyodbc.version)
    try:
        print("Connecting", CONN_STR)
        pyodbc.connect(CONN_STR)
        print("Connected OK")
    except Exception as err:
        print("Failed to connect - is your connection string correct?")
        print(err)
        exit(1)

    print('About to garbage collect')
    gc.collect()
    print('No crash - did you forget to use a Unicode PWD or fix the bug?')
    exit(1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
```

Output on my Windows 7 system, with the [latest 3.0.10 release from pypi](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/pyodbc/3.0.10) installed by pip from the wheel:

```
python version: 2.7.9 (default, Dec 10 2014, 12:24:55) [MSC v.1500 32 bit (Intel)]
pyodbc version: 3.0.10
Connecting PWD=pa££word;DATABASE=test_pyodbc;DRIVER={SQL Server};UID=test_pyodbc_unicode;Server=.\SQLEXPRESS
Connected OK
About to garbage collect
Exception UnicodeEncodeError: UnicodeEncodeError('ascii', u'PWD=pa\xa3\xa3word;DATABASE=test_pyodbc;DRIVER={SQL Server};UID=test_pyodbc_unicode;Server=.\\SQLEXPRESS', 6, 8, 'ordinal not in range(128)') in 'garbage collection' ignored
Fatal Python error: unexpected exception during garbage collection

This application has requested the Runtime to terminate it in an unusual way.
Please contact the application's support team for more information.
```

If we remove the 4 lines of explicit `gc.collect` stuff at the end of `main` and replace with a dummy loop:

```python
    for i in xrange(2):
        print("Fake work", i)
    print("Done")
```

Then python itself doesn't crash, but the `UnicodeEncodeError` is raised when the loop finishes, before we print `Done`:

```
python version: 2.7.9 (default, Dec 10 2014, 12:24:55) [MSC v.1500 32 bit (Intel)]
pyodbc version: 3.0.10
Connecting PWD=pa££word;DATABASE=test_pyodbc;DRIVER={SQL Server};UID=test_pyodbc_unicode;Server=.\SQLEXPRESS

    main()

UnicodeEncodeError: 'ascii' codec can't encode characters in position 6-7: ordinal not in range(128)
```

I can also reproduce with the latest code from master built with `python setup.py bdist_wininst`:

```
python version: 2.7.9 (default, Dec 10 2014, 12:24:55) [MSC v.1500 32 bit (Intel)]
pyodbc version: 3.0.11b8

issue_title: Bug in Stop Limit Order Execution
issue_body:
That is, I sold 2 futures contracts, and I want to buy one contract back when the futures price moves down by 5%, and buy the second one when the price moves down by 10%. I thought this is easy to do. I just created two limit orders with the respective target prices. But when the first limit order is executed, its order status is changed to "Partial", not "Completed"

I noticed that first buy limit order was executed as a buy but with "-1" size.

I think I found where the bug is. In the file position.py, line 128, it should read "opened, closed = 0, size" instead of "opened, closed = 0, -size". Similarly, line 114, it should read "opened, closed = 0, size" instead of "opened, closed = 0, -size"

issue_title: Random UTF-8 string generation must generate valid string
issue_body:
FauxFactory have generated the following string:

```python
u'\u50dd\uf3b6\u1365\u4e5c\u2441\ue94f\u6b18\u4921\u6fc3\u052c'
```

But `\ue94f` [1] and `\uf3b6` [2] are not valid unicode characters. FauxFactory need a better way to generate random unicode strings.

Thanks @Ichimonji10 for helping in the investigation process.

[1] http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/e94f/index.htm
[2] http://www.fileformat.info/info/unicode/char/f3b6/index.htm

issue_title: Incorrect Byte Order Mark in auto_bom()
issue_body:
As defined in http://www.w3.org/TR/REC-xml/#sec-guessing shouldn't the automatically prepended BOM for UTF-8 be "EF BB BF" rather than "EF FF"?  The latter is the appropriate BOM for UTF-16BE encoding.

Also it would be nice to extend the auto BOM feature for other encodings such as UTF-16, etc.

This library has two separate goals actually:
1. provide a clean interface to load and get config values reliably from different sources in the right type with the right fallbacks.
2. make it easy to enrich config items (and potentially sections) with more attributes

Maybe section (class called `Config` now) and item shouldn't be so prominent in the public interface. Instead, maybe there is a place for a wrapper around those two (called `Config` probably) which then does not expose any of the rich underlying functionality.

Maybe there is a place for two separate concepts:
1. config manager (reader, writer, item and section controller)
2. config provider (better name?) (whose only task is to return config values)

Hey,
first of all, this is a great library, saved me a ton of time, thanks a lot!

I noticed a weird quirk that has been bugging me for quite some time - sometimes when I get a template with djangular form and simultaneously get model data for the form, defaults from the form overwrite the model. For example:
 
- I have a field in my django model `name = models.CharField(default="Placeholder")`
- go to a page where I edit the model with template of the rendered djangular form
- in the controller get the model through angular resource
- the name input box shows "Placeholder" instead of the real value and when I log the model, the name attribute is overwritten

When I have the template cached this doesn't happen, so I suspect this only happens when the template gets downloaded after the actual model. Also when I comment out `restoreInputField` from the `ngModel` directive in django-angular.js, this does not happen anymore (but then the defaults stop working in blank forms).

My hacky solution is to copy the model and after a timeout set it back (dealing with the time difference of arrival between the model and the form template), but this quickly gets out of hand. I guess the proper fix would be to check if the model isn't set to anything during restoring the fields. I would submit a PR, but I'm not sure if this is the right way to fix it or if I'm not doing this entirely wrong.

Thanks!

issue_body:
get_cpu_info() always returned None. Some debugging showed that the line 

```
hz_brand = output.split('current CPU frequency is')[1].split('.')[0].lower()
```

in `_get_hz_string_from_beagle_bone` throws an error. In `get_cpu_info_from_proc_cpuinfo` I commented the following block out

```
# Try getting the Hz for a BeagleBone
        if hz_advertised == '0.0':
            scale, hz_advertised = _get_hz_string_from_beagle_bone()
            hz_actual = hz_advertised
```

After that it worked.

issue_title: Ordering a non-continuous segment causes no ordering to happen
issue_body:
I've got a number of py.test suites written that test various aspects of a system.  One file might have a few dozen tests in it, grouped logically.  The next file might have only a dozen.  The next might have a hundred or more.

I'd like to run file1 before file2 before file3, and I absolutely NEED to run the tests inside file1 in a particular order, and the tests inside file2 in a particular order, and same for file3.  So if I start file1 at pytest.mark.run(order=1000) , then 1001, 1002, etc, and file2 at pytest.mark.run(order=2000) and file3 at pytest.mark.run(order=3000) I naively thought that this would result in 1000, 1001, 1002, etc then 2000, 2001, etc, 3000, 3001, etc all getting run in order of increasing order number.

But I'm not seeing that behavior at all when I run the tests as a suite.  In order to get the files to execute in the correct order I've used a script to run py.test on each one of them individually.  And that seemed to work on the files starting at order=1000 or order=2000.  But now that I've got a file starting at order=3000,  I'm seeing strange behavior where instead of

[3000,3001,30002,3003,3004,3005,3006,3007,3008,3009,3010,3011]

I'm instead seeing 

[3008,3009,3010,3011,3000,3001,30002,3003,3004,3005,3006,3007]

I took a peek in https://github.com/ftobia/pytest-ordering/blob/develop/pytest_ordering/__init__.py but it wasn't immediately obvious to me what was getting done where.  I'll probably take another look after I post this to see if I can't put together a pull request, but I'm definitely puzzled.

The main reason I would like this out-of-order business is so that if I add a couple of tests to the end of one file I don't have to go reset the ordering of every other file (by hand!) to make room in the perfectly ordered number line.

issue_title: Provide minimum versions for your requirements
issue_body:
pip-installing jupyter-themes force-installs the latest release for packages like seaborn, which force-installs the latest version of numpy. 

For anaconda users, this can overwrite the mkl-optimized numpy package, and can lead to side-by-side versions of conda ipython and pip ipython.

This can be remedied by specifying minimum required versions for the packages.

issue_title: Version accesses private code since django 1.8
issue_body: This has caused me some issues when resolving GFKs:  in [Version.field_dict](https://github.com/etianen/django-reversion/blob/master/src/reversion/models.py#L183) there is access on `obj._meta.fields`.  Since Django 1.8, when the interface of the Options class was firmed up, this property is marked as [Private API intended only to be used by Django itself](https://github.com/django/django/blob/stable/1.8.x/django/db/models/options.py#L381-L383).   External access should go through `get_fields()` method instead.

issue_title: Allow the introduction of the atomic transaction to be optional.
issue_body:
As weird as it seems, wrapping a section of code inside of an atomic transaction can modify the behaviour of an application using django-reversion.

In Django, the changes made inside of a transaction are kept for the thread that initiated that transaction. So if you change a row, and re-read that from you'll get the modified value. If anyone outside of the transaction does the same thing, they'll see the previous value (unless you commit the transaction obviously).

Now, if an application using django-reversion happens to assume that auto-commit is True (and this is the default), it could modify a row in the DB and then launch a thread that assumes that this change has happened. But if the change was done inside of a transaction, this thread will not see the change (there's a race condition there, but that's another topic). This could introduce problems in the project using this library.

While all of this can be avoided (and it probably should), I think that the user of django-reversion should make that call. Especially if it's a big code base where this simple change can be seen as risky.

Adding a setting for this would be very useful.

I'm using python 3.6 and after installing django-url-filter my test runner is broken. Turns out that it's caused by the enum34 package which is not compatible with the standard library enum, which is used by other standard library modules such as `re`. 

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<string>", line 1, in <module>
  File "<string>", line 3, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/site-packages/execnet/gateway_base.py", line 18, in <module>
    import traceback
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/traceback.py", line 5, in <module>
    import linecache
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/linecache.py", line 11, in <module>
    import tokenize
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/tokenize.py", line 33, in <module>
    import re
  File "/usr/local/lib/python3.6/re.py", line 142, in <module>
    class RegexFlag(enum.IntFlag):
AttributeError: module 'enum' has no attribute 'IntFlag'
```
It seems that there is a package that solves this. It will install enum34 only on versions < 3.4, to avoid messing with the standard library enum in current versions of python 3.

https://pypi.python.org/pypi/enum-compat

issue_title: reset_state fails when combining update_fields and FIELDS_TO_CHECK
issue_body:
When you use FIELDS_TO_CHECK on a model and then save it using update_fields, and update_fields contains a field not in FIELDS_TO_CHECK you get a KeyError because it's trying to pull a field from new_state in reset_state() that is not there.

Quick and dirty repro case:

    class TestModelWithSpecifiedFields(DirtyFieldsMixin, models.Model):
        boolean1 = models.BooleanField(default=True)
        boolean2 = models.BooleanField(default=True)
        FIELDS_TO_CHECK = ['boolean1']

    tm = TestModelWithSpecifiedFields.objects.create()

    tm.boolean1 = False
    tm.boolean2 = False
    
    # This is causing the KeyError
    tm.save(update_fields=["boolean2"])


I'll have a PR up with a fix soon.

issue_title: mutate_value in FloatAttribute not always clamping
issue_body:
Hi. In 0.91's attributes.py, mutate_value in FloatAttribute has the following code:

```python
        if r < replace_rate + mutate_rate:
            mutate_power = getattr(config, self.mutate_power_name)
            return value + gauss(0.0, mutate_power)
```
This return does not clamp the result; the clamping is done (within init_value) if replacing, and if unchanged the final returned result is clamped:
```python
        return self.clamp(value, config)
```
But if the result is mutated (not replaced), it isn't clamped.

-Allen

issue_title: Switching of the inner theme is too slow.
issue_body: Quick preview of function, switching of the inner theme is too slow.

For parsing, unicode all works great in the unit tests, but there's one huge problem: in the unit tests the `stream` object passed to `CSVParser.parse` is a `StringIO`, while in production (apache/wsgi) it's actually an instance of `<class 'django.core.handlers.wsgi.WSGIRequest'>`. The end result is the unit test pass, showing unicode success, but when you POST some unicode to an endpoint using the parser you get the dreaded:

`{"detail":"CSV parse error - 'ascii' codec can't decode byte 0xe2 in position 17136: ordinal not in range(128)"}`

I wrote a stopgap hotfix in a fork (https://github.com/alertmedia/django-rest-framework-csv), but it breaks all the unit tests, which are written using the `StringIO` streams. I also used unicodecsv library, not builtin csv.

Any ideas on reconciliation here?
I don't seem to find a way to have compression enabled for HTTP responses. Could you add that as an option?

issue_title: Wrong AJAX url for admin sorting
issue_body:
Currently for every registered model there is attached url with model_id parameter. All of these URLs will be with the same name and when resolved in template will point to last registered. For example if I have `app1` with models `App1Model1` and `App1Model2` and `app2` with models `App2Model1` and `App2Model2` and all of them are `Sortable` and registered in admin I will have these URLs:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>/

Now I can order all models on all urls. For example if `App1Model2` is with `model_id==2` then  I can order the items in any of this URLs:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/2/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/2/

The same goes for any other model. In fact all models will be sorted in the URL registered last. For example if I register `App2Model2` last then all models will be sorted at the urls under:

    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/<model_id>

This all goes with AJAX and is unnoticed for users but is somehow counter intuitive.
Also there is no permission check which which will restrict access for sorting to people which does not have change permissions to given model.

The correct way of doing this will be to do the sorting for every model in the respective URLs. For example:

    /admin/app1/model1/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app1/model2/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app2/model1/sorting/do-sorting/
    /admin/app2/model2/sorting/do-sorting/

I will try to prepare PR for that.

One question though - Which is the minimum Django version that is supported?

Hello!

I have encountered a major performance issue with this library, due to the generic relation defined in `BaseCommentAbstractModel` 

I was hoping it'd be as simple as adding this to the Meta:

```
index_together = [                                          
    ('content_type', 'object_pk')
]
```

Alas, this does not work on MySQL (not sure if it would or wouldn't on Postgres or other databases), due to the use of a `TextField` for the `object_pk`: 

```
_mysql_exceptions.OperationalError: (1170, "BLOB/TEXT column 'object_pk' used in key specification without a key length")
```

I have already solved this for my own project's needs in a fork by switching to a `CharField`, but I suspect the performance hit (of the missing index) is something other users will encounter, sooner or later, so I'd like to push it upstream.  

The major risk I can see to this is it would be a breaking change for anyone who has `object_pk`s larger than the max_length of a `CharField`, but the stated intention for a non-numeric field is to support UUIDs, which could still be supported.

I will submit a PR if this something that would be accepted.

When [supplying headers using `_request_options`](http://bravado.readthedocs.io/en/latest/advanced.html#adding-request-headers) `bravado`'s validation checks their names with case sensitivity, while it shouldn't, as [HTTP header names are case insensitive](https://www.w3.org/Protocols/rfc2616/rfc2616-sec4.html#sec4.2). 

That leads to `SwaggerMappingError`'s when a header is required and the case sensitivity of its name differs between the OpenAPI specification and the provided header name in the code.

A workaround is to use a `CaseInsensitiveDict` instead of a `dict` for the `headers` in `_request_config` like:
```python
from requests.structures import CaseInsensitiveDict

Pet = client.get_model('Pet')
Category = client.get_model('Category')
pet = Pet(id=42, name="tommy", category=Category(id=24))
swagger_client.pet.addPet(
    body=pet,
    _request_options={"headers": CaseInsensitiveDict(data={"foo": "bar"})},
).result()
```

While that workaround works fine I'd expect `bravado` to handle that internally in a transparent way for the user.

Hi,

 We have noticed a serious performance problem with the way AutoSlugField picks the slugs.

 Please see: https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/blob/master/django_extensions/db/fields/__init__.py#L132

 If the `populate_from` field is empty or contains text which occurs frequently in a table with tens of thousands of records then this function gets stuck in a dumb loop trying to look for the next available number that it can append to the slug.

 We noticed this when it was taking 2000+ DB queries sometimes to make an AutoSlug, as you can imagine this is completely unacceptable (and made me LOL when I finally found the source of all these queries after hours of debugging).

 I propose that instead of a dumb incrementing number the slug should use 24bits of random data (base32 encoded). I know - it's still a query in a loop, but at least we're not trying thousands of numbers in sequence every time it runs.

 The same problem was brought up in a previous bug almost two years ago, but nobody seemed to think of this.

"""
When the field-adding migration is run and the models saved, though, the current form of ASF doesn't take an empty string into account, and generates slugs '', '-2', '-3', etc. I believe the same would happen with null values ('None', 'None-2', 'None-3', etc).

https://github.com/django-extensions/django-extensions/pull/40
"""
```
from base64 import b32encode
import os

...

        while not slug or queryset.filter(**kwargs):
            slug = original_slug
            end = '%s%s' % (self.separator, b32encode(os.urandom(3)).lower().strip('='))
            end_len = len(end)
            if slug_len and len(slug) + end_len > slug_len:
                slug = slug[:slug_len - end_len]
                slug = self._slug_strip(slug)
            slug = '%s%s' % (slug, end)
            kwargs[self.attname] = slug
        return slug
```

issue_title: poller.poll() no longer returns when interrupted by signal and restarts timeout
issue_body:
The code below polls for activity on a socket for 5 seconds and sets up an alarm to interrupt it after 1 second. What used to happen was that poller.poll() would raise zmq.AGAIN after the signal handler returned. With libzmq 4.2.2 and pyzmq 16.0.2 poller.poll() restarts the poll with the initial timeout. The effect of this is that poller.poll() will never timeout in this example.

    #!/usr/bin/python3

    import logging
    import signal
    import zmq

    # enable logging output
    numeric_log_level = getattr(logging, "INFO", None)
    logging.basicConfig(level=numeric_log_level)

    # set up a alarm heartbeat once a second
    def on_alarm(signalnum, frame):
        logging.info("on_alarm()")
        signal.alarm(1)
    signal.signal(signal.SIGALRM, on_alarm)
    signal.alarm(1)

    # create and connect socket
    ctx = zmq.Context()
    socket = ctx.socket(zmq.DEALER)
    socket.connect("inproc://test")
    poller = zmq.Poller()
    poller.register(socket, zmq.POLLIN)

    # poll socket for 5 seconds
    logging.info("polling for 5 seconds")
    poller.poll(5000);
    logging.info("polling done")

    # cleanup
    socket.close()
    ctx.destroy()

issue_title: Add ability to validate/document content of Response instances.
issue_body:
If your logic function returns a `doctor.response.Response` instance in order to set response headers or modify the status code, it's response will not be validated or documented, even if it's a doctor type.

It would be nice to add a Generic to the `Response` type so that the user could specify the type of the content of the Response.  e.g.

```python
from doctor.response import Response
from api import types as t
from api.model import Foo

def get_foo(foo_id: t.FooID) -> Response:
    foo = Foo.get_by_id(foo_id)
    return Response(foo, headers={'X-Custom': 'custom'})
```

This logic had to add a custom header to the response `X-Custom`.  Because of that it will not validate that the response is a valid `t.Foo`, nor will it document the response object.  If we add some code and make Response a `Generic`, we can make this possible.

```python
from doctor.response import Response
from api import types as t
from api.model import Foo

def get_foo(foo_id: t.FooID) -> Response[t.Foo]:
    foo = Foo.get_by_id(foo_id)
    return Response(foo, headers={'X-Custom': 'custom'})
```

https://docs.python.org/3/library/typing.html#user-defined-generic-types

8 months ago we removed the tiling calculator for sound reasons (see https://github.com/gem/oq-engine/pull/2253). However, now we have the `shared_dir` functionality, which has changed the game completely The problem of the tiling was the data transfer, since the same sources had to be transferred multiple times. Now that we have the shared_dir, the source model can be read directly from the workers, thus bypassing the celery/rabbitmq combo. This looks very attractive, especially if the points sources are stored efficiently in .hdf5 format and become fast to read. A tiling calculator would allow for further optimizations, such as avoiding recomputing multiple times the same sources, as it happens in the Canada model. Also, a worker-based new tiling calculator could avoid filtering the sources in the controller node, which was slow. In short, it makes sense to restore it now.

issue_title: TChannel.advertise should be safe to call multiple times
issue_body: Currently, advertise for the sync and async clients do not keep track whether they're already advertising. This means that calling advertise multiple times is unsafe because that'll just start up multiple advertise loops. We should keep track of advertisement status so that it's safe to all advertise multiple times.

var yaml = require('js-yaml');

issue_title: Number of acker executors does not default to the number of worker nodes

if the `-w` option of `sparse submit` is set to some number `N` the number of acker executors is said to be set to `N` as well. So I think the default value for `-a` is not correctly set to the number of workers by default.

issue_title: Move model selection utilities from HTM Engine into NuPIC
issue_body:
As described by an [internal JIRA task](https://jira.numenta.com/browse/ENG-73):

Presently, unicorn's backend relies on `getScalarMetricWithTimeOfDayParams()` from htmengine, which would be better moved into NuPIC.

```python
from htmengine.algorithms.modelSelection.clusterParams import (
  getScalarMetricWithTimeOfDayParams)
```

The plan it to move the `algorithms.modelSelection*` logic and data files somewhere into nupic, where they may be shared by others:

1. move `htmengine/algorithms/modelSelection/*` to `src/nupic/frameworks/opf/common_models/`
1. Another issue is the way it loads the params from disk. https://github.com/numenta/numenta-apps/blob/master/htmengine/htmengine/algorithms/modelSelection/clusterParams.py#L80 does not work when the package is not expanded.
  - Use https://pythonhosted.org/setuptools/pkg_resources.html#resource-extraction because the package will be inside a zip file
  - or pkg_resources.resource_stream

browser.getControl(name="a").displayValue = ['c']
```

silently ignores the unknown values. This results in no selected options in the example above.

I think in version 4.x an error was raised because there is no `displayOption` with the required title. (But I currently doe not code at hand to check this as 4.x calls into `mechanize` to select the value.)
The current behaviour is error prone for typos.

I propose to raise an exception (like `KeyError`) in this case.

Any suggestions?

On recent ubuntu, the boost python library is named

    libboost_python-py35.so

however galsim expects 

    libboost_python3.so

There is a bug for this in ubuntu (https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/boost1.53/+bug/1231609) but it is three years old, so unlikely to be fixed.

I  had to add a sym link.  However on many systems the user won't be able to add the sym link due to privilege restrictions, and compiling boost separately is daunting.

Does it make sense to add a check for this library name?

GalSim does not seem to have a __version__ variable aka many other packages. IMHO this is

aka

import astropy
print('astropy.__version__:  ', astropy.__version__)

After a certain A/B test, the data scientist is often asked about which subset of users is most affected by the new feature. One can actually answer this by building a decision tree (splitting the dataset) to maximize the treatment effect within each node.

In a way, this is kinda like a post-hoc subgroup analysis, but might be more useful. So much for food for thought;-)

argschema not handling non-required Nested schemas with required fields well
issue_body:
```
import argschema
import marshmallow as mm


class MyNested(argschema.schemas.DefaultSchema):
    a = argschema.fields.Int(required=True)
    b = argschema.fields.Str(required=True)

class MySchema(argschema.ArgSchema):
    nested = argschema.fields.Nested(MyNested, only=['a', 'b'],
        required=False, default=mm.missing)

def test_nested_example():
    mod = argschema.ArgSchemaParser(schema_type=MySchema,
                                     args = [])
    assert(not 'nested' in mod.args.keys)
    
def test_nested_marshmallow_example():
    schema = MySchema()
    (result,errors)=schema.load({})
    assert(len(errors)==0)
```

test_nested_example does not pass, but test_nested_marshmallow_example does.  If the user wants to specify a Nested schema which is optional, but if filled in has some required fields, we aren't handling that well now.

Installation of the `master` branch with pip is currently supported by `pip install git+https://github.com/clab/dynet#egg=dynet`.

To support installation by a simple `pip install dynet` (and versioning!), we need to upload a binary distribution package to PyPI. I think it would be a good idea to upload a precompiled .whl file, to reduce installation time significantly. It must be a separate file for Linux, OSX and Windows, compiled on the respective systems. I think this should be done once there is a 2.0 release.

However, I'm pretty sure it won't work with the current configuration where there is a main `setup.py` calling the `python/setup.py` which is generated from `setup.py.in`, because this way the main one doesn't know about the Cython extensions, and they are missing from the created wheel. Therefore, I believe the two `setup.py` files should be combined into a single static one, maybe replacing the CMake `${...}` directives by environment variables or parameters.

The docs say affine_transform(xs) Return an expression equal to: xs[0] + xs[1]*xs[2] + xs[3]*xs[4] + ...
But it's not absolutely right. The dims of two outputs are not equal.
For example, the following code
```
import dynet as dy
m = dy.Model()
_W = m.add_parameters((4,3))
_b = m.add_parameters(4)
x = dy.inputVector([1,2,3])
W = dy.parameter(_W)
b = dy.parameter(_b)
y = b + W * x
z = dy.affine_transform([b, W , x])
print y.dim(), z.dim()
```
will print  `((4,), 1) ((4, 1), 1)`

From https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-datastore/issues/92:

I stumbled upon this while deploying my app for the first time on GKE/GCE.

I had to explicitly specify `project` when creating a client: `gcloud.datastore.Client(project="my-project")`, otherwise I just got an `gcloud.exceptions.ServiceUnavailable: 503 Backend Error`.

First I thought the problem would be that autodetecting the project-id didn't work, but `gcloud.datastore.Client(credentials=credentials, project=project)` returns my project-id, be it as `bytes` instead of `str`.

I quickly verified that using `bytes` as a project-id does not work, i.e. `gcloud.datastore.Client(project=b"my-project")` resulted in that neat `503 Backend Error`.

The docs in this link do not make it clear on how to use the library against the local emulator, with out any creds needed. https://cloud.google.com/datastore/docs/datastore-api-tutorial

The service object can be instantiated without creds for emulator use:
c = datastore.Client(project='test', http=httplib2.Http())

The lib I assume requires the latest version of the emulator to work, it should be noted somewhere in the docs too, I did a "gcloud components install gcd-emulator" to get the latest version.

Lastly, with all the above in place, the lib does not work with the emulator, it calls a url that the emulator does not recognise and therefore complains. I had to make this adjustment to have the lib and emulator working together:

Change  https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/gcloud-python/blob/master/gcloud/datastore/connection.py line 49 
from:
API_URL_TEMPLATE = ('{api_base}/{api_version}/projects'
to:
API_URL_TEMPLATE = ('{api_base}/datastore/{api_version}/projects'

With this change, the library and latest emulator worked together without creds, as expected.

I am not sure if this is a general issue with the lib, or if I am using it incorrectly to need this, or if the emulator is the issue and it should be fixed there?

issue_body:
In GAE standard, ndb has the concept of the urlsafe string version of a Key. 
e.g. `url_string = sandy_key.urlsafe()` produces a result like `agVoZWxsb3IPCxIHQWNjb3VudBiZiwIM`.

However, I cannot find a way to use this format with the google.cloud.datastore library for code running outside GAE standard. Please add a method to the Key class to create urlsafe strings and a method to generate Keys from urlsafe strings. Alternatively, provide some documentation on how to decode/encode them with the existing library.

NB, the [console support page](https://support.google.com/cloud/answer/6365503?hl=en) says "The encoding method is available in any Cloud Datastore client library."

issue_title: Consider adding a `desc` or `descending` keyword argument to `Query.order_by`
issue_body:
From Google internal bug 65127895 (intentionally not providing a URL).

----

Currently the sort order should be specified as a string:

```python
query = cities_ref.order_by('name', direction='DESCENDING').limit(3)
```

This is error prone, since a user may make a typo while typing the
string out. The following would be much simpler:

```python
cities_ref.order_by('name', desc=True)
```

We (the Firestore team) don't have a strong opinion on this. Note that the Java SDK uses an Enum, the JS SDK uses the string and the iOS SDK uses a boolean. So feel free to do what you think is more idiomatic.

/cc @schmidt-sebastian

Hi clowwindy,

Can you please add support for "Ignore Hosts" in sslocal? 
Just like the ones in the Linux system network proxy preferences.

I am asking for this feature because we need to use the shadowsocks' socks5 proxy in some software directly rather than the system configured proxy, but we want some network traffics (especially  localhost) can get bypassed in the socks proxy. 

To get it clearer, here is my use case:
My goal: send emails to an SMTP server in my Java program.
My problem: the SMTP protocol is blocked by the firewall.
My solutions: use socks proxy to bypass the firewall, so I will run my program with `java -DsocksProxyHost=127.0.0.1 -DsocksProxyPort=9876 ...`.
New problem: all TCP connections will go through the socks proxy, even mysql (jdbc:mysql://localhost:3306/xxx) and postgresql (jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/yyy) requests, then my program fails.

It would be awesome if we could support setting ignore hosts in the conf file in shadowsocks.

Reference:
[How do I configure JavaMail to work through my proxy server? ](http://www.oracle.com/technetwork/java/javamail/faq/index.html#proxy)
It's said that I can set [`mail.smtp.socks.host` and `mail.smtp.socks.port`](https://javamail.java.net/nonav/docs/api/com/sun/mail/smtp/package-summary.html) in my java program. But apparently [org.apache.commons.mail](https://commons.apache.org/proper/commons-email/) does not support it. 

Many thanks,

issue_title: pex doesn't properly handle PEP 496 Environment Markers for cross-platform builds
issue_body:
a case noted in our monorepo was:

```
pex: Failed to resolve a requirement: The 'appnope; sys_platform == "darwin"' distribution was not found and is required by ipython    
```

where a user had attempted to build a pex on linux to be run on OSX that included `ipython==5.4.1`, but the transitive ipython -> appnope linkage used a PEP496 environmental marker that was evaluated on the buildtime platform (linux) causing the `appnope` dep not to be included in the pex for OSX runtime. when building the pex on an OSX machine, the problem does not surface.

this seems to indicate that we'll need to somehow parameterize the PEP 496 Environment Markers on each resolve round with synthetic per-target-platform values.

issue_title: Translated python bdists should be cached in a separate cache
issue_body:
When python calls pex to translate sdist into bdist, the translated bdist is written to the global cache dir and is then used as a bdist to re-resolve.

If we enable compiling sdists, then disable it, the cached results will be used to resolve which may be unintended. As a result, allowing sdists to be compiled is only allowed at the python_setup level and not allowed to be configured at the target level.
This is needed by all node and/or npm using tasks.
breakout task from the v2 FE HLD [here](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1Hn73YlhTPROlULTMa_3A-Fdv7hAPiHFII8rvXri5l7E/edit#)

issue_title: Reading dicomdir may fail if dcm files are in a subfolder
issue_body:
If provided with a directory,  `_can_read` function of `dicom` plugin tries to open all files in the directory in sequence until it finds first dicom file.

This fails if the directory contains a subdirectory and that subdirectory has name that after sorting comes before first dicom file. 

Code tries to open directory with `open` and raises `IOError: [Errno 21] Is a directory: ...`

Related code:
```python
    def _can_read(self, request):
        # If user URI was a directory, we check whether it has a DICOM file
        if os.path.isdir(request.filename):
            files = os.listdir(request.filename)
            files.sort()  # Make it consistent
            if files:
                with open(os.path.join(request.filename, files[0]), 'rb') as f:
                    first_bytes = read_n_bytes(f, 140)
                return first_bytes[128:132] == b'DICM'
            else:
                return False
        # Check
        return request.firstbytes[128:132] == b'DICM'
```

For example dicom case with the shown file hierarchy always raises error:
```
data
├── 4PQUVRCL
│   ├── 1CEDXV2R
│   │   ├── I1000000
│   │   ├── ...
│   │   ├── I9900000
│   │   └── VERSION
│   └── VERSION
├── DICOMDIR
├── LOCKFILE
└── VERSION
```

Using this mp4: https://www.dropbox.com/s/vvou73bjf27t2ds/black-2.mp4?dl=0 and `avconv`, when I try to read I get:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/imageio/core/functions.py", line 111, in get_reader
    return format.get_reader(request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/imageio/core/format.py", line 158, in get_reader
    return self.Reader(self, request)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/imageio/core/format.py", line 207, in __init__
    self._open(**self.request.kwargs.copy())
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/imageio/plugins/ffmpeg.py", line 287, in _open
    self._load_infos()
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/imageio/plugins/ffmpeg.py", line 463, in _load_infos
    fps = float(line[match.start():match.end()].split(' ')[1])
AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'start'
```

It would be nice to be able to handle preferences for plugins.
For example, `tifffile.py` should be always used as the default plugin for TIF files, if available, since it handles the widest assortment of TIF foibles.
Also, the user should be able to specify the desired plugin three ways:
- A global default
- A default for a given file extension
- As a keyword argument to `imread` or `imwrite`

issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Currently, searching results in some very unexpected behaviors. It seems to be "fuzzy" but in the wrong ways - e.g. it will pick up on completely unrelated content due to spelling mappings, while not finding synonyms of common words.

### Expected behavior

Search should at least be predictable: if something appears in the results, it should be obvious why it's there. This will encourage people to trust and use it.

As a simple starting point, we should remove the behavior which causes results to appear based on complex character mappings.

This will reduce the number of results for each search query, but make what does get returned more likely to be relevant to the user.

### User-facing consequences

Search is confusing and misleading because it often returns completely unrelated content

issue_title: Creating NICs has incorrect handling of Booleans
issue_body:
When NICs are created via `create_nic()` or `create_server()` the Boolean values for the properties `dhcp` and `firewall_active` are correctly handled.
E.g. setting `dhcp=False` has no effect. The created NIC will end up with `dhcp=True`.

Creation of NICs uses `_create_nic_dict()` which maps a NIC object's properties to JSON format.
For Boolean properties this is performed like
```
if nic.dhcp:
    properties['dhcp'] = nic.dhcp
```
So, if the property is False, the mapping is skipped.

For now we do not store any metadata in the storage (bigquery, sql etc). It means `pull_datapackage` works only using reflection of the database. So additional metadata can't be restored (description, user defined fields etc).

As a solution we can store additional table with stringified `datapackage.json`.

---
**pwalsh**

@roll excellent observation. Seeing as we can't rely on a backend supporting, for example, JSON storage, and neither can we expect any particular fields on any given data package, I agree that we can just have a table with a stringified datapackage.json, but it might have other columns too, that for example point to the tables used for the DP in question (Imagine a storage backend that holds data from many datapackages - we might expect a common meta table that points out to the various tables for each package).

doesn't raise any exception.

`validate` should be more strict

Kind regards

In the docs this is the procedure to do a manual update:

> $ ssh core@<server ip>
> $ sudo su
> $ echo GROUP=stable > /etc/coreos/update.conf
> $ systemctl unmask update-engine.service
> $ systemctl start update-engine.service
> $ update_engine_client -update
> $ systemctl stop update-engine.service
> $ systemctl mask update-engine.service
> $ reboot

this is wrong (at least now).
Why? https://github.com/coreos/bugs/issues/262

the boot partition is updated after the reboot so if the update-engine service is disabled it will not run and update the boot flags.
Only after this output from `journalctl -u update-engine.service`
`[0226/232543:INFO:update_attempter.cc(674)] Updating boot flags...`
is safe to disable the update-engine service. (after the next update it will use the updated version)

reference https://github.com/coreos/bugs/issues/262

issue_title: Old error in the SBCharSetProber.cpp (or .py) of the  Universal Charset Detector
Trinity does not limit total peer connections
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

Now that we are *useful* and staying connected to peers, a long running trinity node will acquire a **lot** of peer connections inbound, eventually swamping the node's connection.


### How can it be fixed

Finish https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/824

issue_title: Add new gas and return data API to Computation object.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

With the introduction of the `REVERT` opcode in the byzantium fork rules we can no longer assume that the presence of `computation.error` means that gas has been fully depleted or that there is no return data.

This has resulted in the following lines of code being repeated in various places.

* https://github.com/pipermerriam/py-evm/blob/piper/byzantium/evm/vm/computation.py#L247
* https://github.com/pipermerriam/py-evm/blob/piper/byzantium/evm/vm/computation.py#L256
* https://github.com/pipermerriam/py-evm/blob/piper/byzantium/evm/logic/call.py#L114
* https://github.com/pipermerriam/py-evm/blob/piper/byzantium/evm/logic/call.py#L122
* https://github.com/pipermerriam/py-evm/blob/piper/byzantium/evm/vm/computation.py#L165


Some of this logic repetition would be better served by a dedicated API on the `Computation` object.

### How can it be fixed

Add the following two new APIs to the `Computation` object.

```python
def.should_burn_gas():
    return self.error and self.error.burns_gas

def should_erase_return_data():
    return self.error and self.error.zeros_return_data
```

*if* you would also like to cleanup the slighly confusing name `zeros_return_data` and change it to `erases_return_data` go for it.

issue_title: HexaryTrieSync._existing_nodes may use a ton of memory
issue_body: That is a cache we use to speed things up, but it has no limit on how much it can grow, and for a full state sync that may end up consuming a lot of memory. See https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/pull/1124#discussion_r208231117 for more info

issue_body:
* Version: 4.3.0

```
How to convert with web3py address to Vyper Address uint256 ?

* Version: 4.5
* Python: 3.5
* OS: win


### What was wrong?
impossible to connect to infura using websockets

I am using this to connect to infura:

from web3 import Web3
web3 = Web3(Web3.WebsocketProvider("wss://ropsten.infura.io/ws"))
web3.eth.blockNumber

No response, the console just become unresponsive. Tried two different computers in different networks, same result. Also used rinkeby and mainnet, same result.
https://github.com/pypa/auditwheel allows to create precompiled wheels supporting different linux distros. Maybe we can utilize it to modify the wheels while the wagon is created

issue_title: Reading derivations from raw tsdb result files doesn't work due to double backslashes
issue_body:
Hi Michael!

I was trying to read in derivations directly from the raw tsdb result files because the `delphin.itsdb` module was too slow (perhaps worthy of an issue within itself?) and I found out that those derivations will not load with `delphin.derivation.Derivation` because they are double backslash escaped, where as the module expects single backslash escaped.

issue_title: Issue with installing R's Bioconductor packages
issue_body:
I'm attempting to install the package `BiRewire` via Bioconductor, but it's returning an error. This error only occurs on an installation of R that was installed via `conda`; the error does not occur when it's a standalone installation of R. Any ideas? 

```

opened URL

downloaded 339 KB

** libs

```

It's _possible_ this is a version mismatch issue, though I hope not. I am currently using R 3.1.3, but I would like to update to 3.2 (which is what bioconductor and BiRewire want me to do). Unfortunately, there's not a complete set of R 3.2 packages built in the conda channels yet (for example, the official Anaconda.org channel is still 3.1, I believe).

Thanks!

https://github.com/conda/conda/pull/2526#issuecomment-220751869

Since this type of bug was introduced by at least three people independently, I think it would be good to add a test for this. The test could be something along the lines of:

    tmp_dir = tempfile.mkdtemp()
    shutil.copyfile(conda.install.__file__, tmp_dir)
    sys.path = [tmp_dir] # we also need the standard library here
    import install

Basically, put the `install` module into an empty directory, and test if it can be imported by itself.

issue_title: "conda update --file pkgs.txt" results in "Error: no package names supplied"
issue_body:
This is a weird result:

```bash
[root@silver ~]# /opt/wakari/anaconda/bin/conda update -p /opt/wakari/anaconda --file install.txt 
Error: no package names supplied
# If you want to update to a newer version of Anaconda, type:
#
# $ conda update --prefix /opt/wakari/anaconda anaconda

[root@silver ~]# cat install.txt 
bokeh=0.9.2=np19py27_0
```

I thought maybe it was because `install.txt` had version information, so I created `update.txt` without version info and tried again, but same result.

```bash

Currently, version parsing and ordering are accomplished in conda via the [MatchSpec](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L40), [VersionSpec](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/version.py#L253), [VersionOrder](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/version.py#L19), and [Package](https://github.com/conda/conda/blob/master/conda/resolve.py#L119) classes.

I thought I would open discussion on using PyPA's `packaging` module to handle parsing/ordering/comparing versions in conda.  There are a few pros and cons that need to be considered though.

Documentation: https://packaging.pypa.io/en/latest/
Repository: https://github.com/pypa/packaging

Pros:
* `packaging` implements [PEP440](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0440/) and [PEP508](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0508/).  The current versioning classes in conda seem to implement the older PEP380.
* code reuse is usually a good idea
* maintained by [PyPA](https://github.com/pypa), who also maintain pip
* Useful abstractions for dealing with versions and version ordering
* Code is neatly organized and well documented
* Covers as many versioning schemes as I can think of

Cons:
* `packaging` depends on `six` and `pyparsing`.  Conda strives to have a few external dependencies as it can.
* Initial implementation might take a lot of work, but hopefully not too much.  The version handling classes have been in conda for a long time.  Rooting out all of the assumptions and peculiarities throughout the conda codebase might take time.
* Could possibly break other code if other people have depended on conda's internal classes.  Hopefully, this is a non-issue, but it's listed here for completeness sake.
* The way conda does things seems to work

This issue is for discussion.  Is this a desirable change for the conda community?  Why or why not?  There are also a few other libraries that handle version parsing and ordering, though none seem to be as mature as `packaging`.

Requirements:

issue_title: Exit status of 99 for "No indices to act on" is inconvenient, make optional
issue_body:
As a rule we run all production shell scripts with exit-on-error enabled (we use [stringent](https://github.com/pottmi/stringent.sh/blob/master/stringent.sh). This has saved us from significant problems on many occasions.

I've recently updated our curator script to use curator v3 and discovered that the script, which contains several curator commands, will sometimes abort part way though due to a curator command reporting "No indices matched provided args." and returning a non-zero status.

Please add an option to return a 0 exit status in the "No indices to act on" case.

issue_title: Get window dimension in global scope will cause issues when az is not run in a terminal
issue_body:
`$TERM` is missing when the command is not run in a terminal. Therefore fetching dimension will fail no matter what. Running `get_window_dim` in global scope will cause an exception when simply `azure-cli-interactive` is just imported.

https://github.com/Azure/azure-cli/blob/4e25d149cf8b1ad2182e89a0c1c29e2638a5b6b8/src/command_modules/azure-cli-interactive/azclishell/gather_commands.py#L16

issue_title: Application Gateway frontend ip update is not working
issue_body:
Update on frontend ip returns immediately and does not set values. See debug output below. Also please note that when a static internal ip is assigned the private ip allocation method should be dynamic. In output below, it is being sent as Dynamic. So even if the update was working, it would send incorrect params to the backend.

issue_title: az aks commands use a different location for .kube directory than kubectl (Windows)
issue_body:
**Describe the bug**
`az aks` commands are using a different path to the configuration for `kubectl` than `kubectl` itself does. 
* `az aks get-credentials` and `az aks browse` are using the `%USERPROFILE%` path
* `kubectl` uses `%HOMEDRIVE%%HOMEPATH%`
If these paths are different `kubectl` does not find the configuration from `az aks get-credentials` and the configuration has to be manually synced between the two paths.
And as `az aks browse` uses `kubectl` to open the connection `az aks browse` fails to run if the file `%HOMEDRIVE%%HOMEPATH%/.kube/config` does not exist (although a configuration file is passed as parameter to `kubectl`, it still tries to access this file )

**To Reproduce**
1. Delete the .kube directories in `%USERPROFILE%` (and in `%HOMEDRIVE%%HOMEPATH%` if different from `%USERPROFILE%`)
2. Change the HOMEDRIVE and HOMEPATH user variables to be different from the USERPROFILE
3. run `az aks get-credentials`
4. run `kubectl get pods`
kubectl errors out as it is unable to find the config file

**Expected behavior**
The path to the `.kube` directory should be identical for `az aks` commands and `kubectl`

**Environment summary**
Install Method: MSI
CLI version: 2.0.32
OS Version: Microsoft Windows 10 Enterprise (10.0.10586)
Shell Type: cmd.exe

**Additional context**
None

Issue #889 provides example code of attempting to find the nearest point on a `btCollisionObject` to a point.  The point is modeled as a zero-radius sphere.  It illustrates that as the point moves closer to the `btCollisionObject` the normal direction reverses direction.

The sphere is *mistakenly* initialized with a quaternion of <1, 0, 0, 0> instead of the more meaningful "identity" quaternion <0, 0, 0, 1>.  In fact, correcting the quaternion resolves the observed behavior.

The bug is that because the query point is modeled as a sphere, the answer shouldn't change.  Spheres are rotation invariant; but bullet does not exhibit this trait.

issue_title: Client hangs on read if no EOF is received
issue_body:
## Long story short

If the server sends no EOF in response, the `response.read()` hangs.
This is actually a speculation from reading aiohttp code, the cause might be different, but there is an easy way to reproduce it. 
Also `requests` library handles the same scenarios successfully.

## Expected behaviour

The `Content-Length` header is provided in the response and thus the client should know how many bytes to read exactly.

## Actual behaviour

The `response.read()` hangs indefinitely.

## Steps to reproduce

```python
import asyncio

import aiohttp
import aiohttp.web
import requests

async def client():
    async with aiohttp.ClientSession(headers={"Range": "bytes=0-1000"}) as session:
        # async with session.get("https://www.airbnb.com") as response:
        async with session.get("https://www.tripadvisor.com") as response:
            await response.read()
            print("Never reached")

print(requests.get("https://www.tripadvisor.com", headers={"Range": "bytes=0-1000"}).text)
print(requests.get("https://www.airbnb.com", headers={"Range": "bytes=0-1000"}).text)

asyncio.get_event_loop().run_until_complete(client())
```

## Your environment

aiohttp==3.3.2
requests==2.19.1
macOS 10.13.5
no proxy
aiohttp client

issue_title: Deprecate returning web.HTTPException from handlers and middlewares
issue_body:
Now an endpoint might either return `web.HTTPException` (`HTTPForbidden` for example) or raise it.

It makes a mess, very often a middleware should check for type of response and process it as exception if the response is an instance of `HTTPException`.

Deprecation could be added just before this line: https://github.com/aio-libs/aiohttp/blob/master/aiohttp/web_protocol.py#L428

Next step in transition plan is adding `HTTPException.make_response()` method.

At the end we could drop inheritance from `web.Response` at all.

@tailhook told me about the problem a year ago.
`aiohttp.web` was designed after Pyramid framework. 
I still think Pyramid is much better designed than Django and Flask but the library is not such popular because alternatives acquired much better promotion.
Anyway, Pyramid inherits HTTP exceptions from both `Response` and `Exception` classes, we did the same.
Now, after years of working on aiohttp and derived projects, it's clean for me: the choice was definitely wrong.

I was not ready for such breaking change at the moment but now I have a strong feeling that the change makes sense and very important.

Sure, we should do transition smoothly: a warning and `make_response()` method could be added in next aiohttp 3.0 release but `web.Response` removal should wait for a year and half at least.

Champions for first two steps are welcome!

We can create **ClientSession** with the argument '*raise_for_status*'.
But what about making two **request** from one application, when first will be with '*raise_for_status* = **True**', second with '*raise_for_status* = **False**'?

In this case we should to append two ClientSession to application.
```python
application['client_session'] = ClientSession()  # default is False
application['other_client_session'] = ClientSession(raise_for_status=True)
```
 I think that this way isn't good.

To solve this problem you can expand '**request**' method with '_raise_for_status_' argument who will redefine ClientSession's '_raise_for_status_' state for this **request**.
```python
async with client_session.request(method, url, raise_for_status=True) as r:
    ...
```

issue_title: The asterisk for "busy cell" is ambiguous
issue_body:
When a cell is displayed as "busy" with an asterisk, it can mean one of several things:

1. The cell is currently executing.
2. An "execute" command was sent to the cell, but another cell is currently executing. The cell will execute when its turn comes.
3. The kernel was interrupted / restarted while the cell was executing, it is currently not executing.

(There might be another scenario that I'm missing here.)
This ambiguity makes it hard to handle notebooks with cells that take some time to execute and are sometimes queued or interrupted. It's hard to understand the current execution status. Often I find myself returning to a notebook and not remembering if the cell is still executing or not.

It would be great to have some way to distinguish the above cases, maybe by using a different symbol for each one instead of using an asterisk for all of them.

issue_title: "Busy" status in the page title disappearing after save
issue_body:
Hi,

If one run a computation in a cell, there is a "(Busy)" added in the title, which is useful when browsing another tab, to see when the computation has finished.

If one then save the notebook (while the computation is running), the added "(Busy)" in the title disappear, while the computation is still running.

I don't think this is some expected behavior.

As far as I see it is not possible to copy/paste cells between different notebooks.

This can be a very time saving feature for someone who needs to edit multiple notebooks at the same time (e.g. creating new notebooks from existing ones). 

As an example, I am using Jupyter as a lecturer. When I restructure course materials (notebooks), I have to recreate cells for the new notebook(s) even if those cells are already in my existing notebooks.
